company.name: Extra Space Storage
company.description: Extra Space Storage is a self-storage REIT that owns, operates, and manages storage properties in the United States.

overview.title: Self-storage platform
paragraphs: Extra Space Storage is a self-administered and self-managed REIT focused on self-storage properties, with a national customer-facing footprint across personal storage, vehicle storage, boat and RV storage, and business storage.

The company combines owned stores, joint ventures, and a large third-party management business, making its moat partly a real estate footprint and partly an operating platform for pricing, customer acquisition, insurance, store management, and acquisitions.

overview.title: Current snapshot
paragraphs: Extra Space reported 2025 total revenues of $3.38 billion, net income of $1.02 billion, and funds from operations attributable to common stockholders and unit holders of $1.75 billion.
The company operated 4,281 stores at December 31, 2025 and managed 1,856 stores for third-party owners, while CompaniesMarketCap reported a June 2026 market capitalization near $32.07 billion.

moatNarrative: Extra Space has a strong physical and operating moat because self-storage rewards local facility density, convenient locations, security, brand trust, pricing systems, digital move-in workflows, and capital access for acquisitions. Its ManagementPlus platform also lets the company earn fees and build operating data beyond wholly owned properties.

The moat is not absolute. The storage unit itself is standardized square footage, and demand is local enough that customers can switch among nearby facilities or use spare rooms, garages, warehouses, and cooperative storage when trust and access requirements are lower.
decentralizationNarrative: The core service is moderately decentralizable because many storage jobs are local matching problems: households and small businesses need nearby, secure, flexible space. Underused residential, community, and commercial space can compete with purpose-built REIT facilities for lower-risk and shorter-duration demand.

The difficult parts are insurance, liability, access control, climate control, fire and zoning compliance, customer support, and dispute resolution. Decentralized or cooperative models can pressure edge cases and third-party management economics, but professionally operated facilities retain advantages for mainstream consumer and business storage.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 251-275; rankApprox is set to 269 based on Extra Space Storage's position in the intake roster and refreshed with public sources accessed on June 24, 2026.

moat.rationale: Large national scale, owned and managed store density, digital customer acquisition, revenue management, tenant insurance economics, acquisition capacity, and third-party management relationships create a strong self-storage moat, moderated by local substitutability of basic storage space.

decentralizability.rationale: Local spare-space marketplaces, cooperative microfacilities, and open facility software can decentralize some storage demand and independent-operator management, but secure access, insurance, climate control, liability, and professional operations limit full replacement.

profitability.rationale: Extra Space reported $1.02 billion of 2025 net income, $973.999 million of net income attributable to common stockholders, $1.75 billion of FFO, and a reported June 2026 operating margin near 31.5%, supporting a high profitability score.

peRatio.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported a June 2026 trailing P/E ratio of approximately 32.8251; this is treated as a rough market-data input because REIT valuation is often assessed with FFO rather than GAAP earnings alone.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported Extra Space Storage's market capitalization at about $32.07 billion as of June 2026.

products.name: Extra Space Storage units
products.summary: Extra Space Storage rents storage units with different sizes, access types, and features, including drive-up, indoor, climate-controlled, vehicle, boat, RV, personal, and business storage.
products.whyItMatters: Storage units are the company's core recurring real estate product, converting convenient local space, security, access control, and customer acquisition into rent, tenant insurance, and management economics.
replacementSketch: A decentralized replacement would not need to copy every professional facility. It could start with verified spare rooms, garages, commercial back rooms, community buildings, and small warehouses for low-risk storage needs.

Purpose-built facilities would remain important for high-value goods, climate-sensitive inventory, frequent access, and customers who prefer standardized security, predictable operations, and institutional insurance.

alternatives.name: Sharetribe-based local storage marketplace
alternatives.summary: A community, cooperative, or local operator could adapt marketplace software to list spare rooms, garages, warehouse bays, and neighborhood storage lockers instead of routing all demand to REIT-owned facilities.

openness.rationale: Sharetribe Go has public source code, while the current Sharetribe platform is commercial; this makes it a hybrid enabling primitive rather than a fully open protocol replacement.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Marketplace software can distribute storage supply across many local hosts and organizations, though discovery, payments, dispute resolution, and reputation can still centralize at the platform layer.

readiness.rationale: Marketplace tooling is mature enough for pilots, but storage-specific workflows such as inspections, key custody, insurance, claims, and restricted access would need custom implementation.

costLeverage.rationale: Using underutilized local space can avoid dedicated facility capex for some demand, but insurance, host incentives, verification, access support, and disputes could absorb much of the savings.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated neighborhood storage market
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federated local-storage network would let households, churches, small warehouses, cooperatives, and local businesses list unused secure space through interoperable marketplaces instead of forcing renters into REIT-owned facilities. Open mapping and marketplace software would handle discovery, while local operators handle inspection, access, and insurance standards.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept changes self-storage from a capital-heavy facility ownership model into a local capacity coordination model, pressuring lower-security and short-duration storage demand.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through interoperable local marketplaces, open location data, and portable reputation rather than through Bitcoin. The key mechanism is allowing many independent operators to publish inventory without one national owner controlling supply.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Hosts publish location, space type, access windows, price, photos, and insurance terms; renters search by distance and requirements; local validators or cooperatives inspect spaces and maintain trust registries shared across federated nodes.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Cheating is constrained through verified address records, inspection photos, escrowed payments, renter reviews, host bonds, insurance certificates, and dispute logs shared across participating marketplaces. Weakness remains around private-property access, undisclosed hazards, and post-rental damage claims.
failureModes: Insurance and liability costs make informal spare-space hosting uneconomic.
Bad access experiences or disputes destroy trust faster than local supply can grow.
Municipal rules, leases, or homeowners associations restrict commercial storage use in residential spaces.
adoptionPath: Start with low-risk categories such as seasonal goods, empty boxes, and non-sensitive furniture in dense neighborhoods.
Add cooperative inspections, standard host agreements, and insurance partnerships before expanding to higher-value or business inventory.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The supply side can be distributed across many local hosts and organizations, with federated discovery reducing reliance on one facility owner.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Marketplace and mapping primitives exist, but storage-specific verification, insurance, and access workflows are harder than ordinary classifieds or rentals.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: A pilot is feasible with existing marketplace tooling, but scaling safely requires inspections, claims handling, host vetting, access procedures, and local compliance work.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The model could pressure small-unit and short-duration demand, but it is unlikely to displace climate-controlled, drive-up, high-security, or professionally managed storage quickly.

disruptionConcepts.name: Cooperative modular storage microfacilities
disruptionConcepts.summary: Neighborhood cooperatives could deploy smaller modular storage rooms, lockers, or converted underused buildings with shared management software and member governance, turning some storage demand from a REIT-owned property product into local infrastructure.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: This would not eliminate large facilities, but it could reduce centralized ownership in dense neighborhoods where small, trusted, nearby storage nodes are enough.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is cooperative ownership and open operational standards. Bitcoin is not central; the pressure comes from local capital formation and shared governance of small storage assets.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Members finance or lease local space, publish available units through open directories, vote on pricing and access rules, and use shared software for reservations, billing, maintenance, and incident reporting.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Trust depends on member identity checks, access logs, tamper-evident locks, periodic audits, insurance coverage, and transparent cooperative records. Collusion or poor maintenance is constrained by member voting and audit trails, but small cooperatives can still fail operationally.
failureModes: Local cooperatives may lack the capital, discipline, or insurance coverage to maintain secure facilities.
Open hardware and access systems can be standardized, but physical security still requires competent local operations.
Customer convenience may be worse than a national chain with many nearby sites and mature digital move-in workflows.
adoptionPath: Begin with apartment buildings, community land trusts, and small-business districts that already have shared governance or unused rooms.
Standardize modular lockers, access control, insurance templates, and open directory listings so new cooperative nodes can be added without designing each site from scratch.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Shared local ownership can decentralize asset control and governance, though each microfacility is still physically centralized at a site level.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Cooperative governance can work for local infrastructure, but storage requires operational reliability, security, and insurance discipline that many informal groups will lack.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Small pilots are plausible in controlled buildings or community organizations, but broad rollout needs standardized hardware, compliance playbooks, and professional operations.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This could serve niche local demand and reduce some household dependence on REIT facilities, but Extra Space's scale, brand, and purpose-built assets remain strong for mainstream demand.

products.name: Life Storage
products.category: Acquired self-storage brand and portfolio integration
products.summary: Life Storage is the acquired self-storage platform that Extra Space folded into its store portfolio and current web presence after the 2023 merger.
products.whyItMatters: The Life Storage deal shows how self-storage scale is built through acquisition, brand migration, third-party management, and operating-system leverage rather than through a differentiated physical unit alone.
replacementSketch: A credible replacement is not a new national brand copying Life Storage. It would give independent storage owners and local cooperatives open tools for facility records, reservations, pricing, maintenance, mapping, and customer workflows.
The goal would be to let small operators share enough software, standards, and verification to compete with the operating-platform advantages of a national consolidator while keeping ownership local.

alternatives.name: openMAINT
alternatives.summary: openMAINT is an open source property and facility management CMMS that can support owner-controlled asset records, maintenance processes, spaces, and facilities operations.

openness.rationale: openMAINT is explicitly positioned as an open source property and facility management solution.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Self-hosted facility records improve operator control and portability, though the software itself does not create a full decentralized storage marketplace.

readiness.rationale: The project maps to property and facility management needs, but replacing a mature storage management platform would require storage-specific reservations, access, insurance, and revenue-management integrations.

costLeverage.rationale: Open CMMS software can reduce license and switching costs for facility records, while customer acquisition, access systems, labor, and insurance remain material operating costs.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open storage operator cooperative
disruptionConcepts.summary: Independent self-storage owners could federate around open facility records, shared listings, maintenance workflows, customer intake, and cooperative insurance or procurement rather than outsourcing their operating stack to a national consolidator. The model targets management-platform economics more than the physical unit itself.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept weakens acquisition and third-party management moats by giving small storage operators a shared operating layer that preserves local ownership while improving customer discovery, maintenance discipline, and back-office efficiency.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federated listings, cooperative governance, and owner-controlled operating data. Bitcoin is not central; the main trust problem is whether independent operators can share records, standards, and reputation without surrendering control to one platform.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Facility owners publish availability, pricing, access rules, and maintenance status through interoperable feeds; cooperative members share software templates, customer support standards, procurement contracts, and audit rules; local customers discover sites through open maps and marketplace nodes.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: False listings and poor operations are constrained by signed facility records, verified addresses, inspection photos, access logs, insurance certificates, maintenance histories, customer reviews, and cooperative sanctions. The weak points are revenue manipulation, inconsistent service quality, and slow dispute resolution.
failureModes: Small operators may not coordinate strongly enough to match Extra Space's revenue management, marketing, and customer support.
Insurance, access control, and claims workflows may remain too specialized for a lightly governed cooperative.
Federated listings can fragment unless standards for availability, pricing, and reputation are simple enough for many operators to adopt.
adoptionPath: Begin with independent storage owners that already use third-party software but want stronger data portability and pooled marketing.
Add open facility records, shared map listings, inspection templates, and cooperative procurement before attempting full reservation and claims workflows.
Use regional operator associations or cooperatives to enforce service standards and portable reputation.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model keeps ownership and operating records distributed across independent facilities while coordinating shared standards through a cooperative or federated network.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open facility software and marketplace primitives exist, but aligning many independent storage owners around pricing, service standards, insurance, and support is difficult.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The first software layer is feasible with open CMMS, ERP, mapping, and marketplace tools, but storage-specific access control, claims, payments, and customer support need integration work.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept can pressure third-party management fees and independent-owner dependence, but it is unlikely to quickly match Extra Space's brand, acquisition pipeline, and national operating scale.

summaryMarkdown: Prepared a publishable Extra Space Storage refresh with two products: Extra Space Storage units and Life Storage integration. The bundle uses the 2025 10-K, current company pages, CompaniesMarketCap valuation inputs, and conservative self-storage decentralization concepts.
